cover.title: What is __Networking?__ OSI Model Explained
cover.hook: Do you know how your data actually travels across the internet?

slides.label: What is Networking?
slides.heading: The System That __Connects Everything__

body.text: Networking is the system of **interconnected devices sharing data** — computers, phones, servers, routers — all communicating over wires, Wi-Fi, or fibre.
Think of it as a global postal system: your data is a letter, IP addresses are house numbers, routers are sorting offices, and protocols are the delivery rules.
**Every single cyber attack travels over a network.** You cannot hack — or defend — what you do not understand.

slides.time: 20 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 Video 1

slides.label: Why OSI Matters
slides.method: WHY — Why Does the OSI Model Matter?
slides.heading: Why OSI is Your Hacker's Bible

body.text: The OSI model is a **7-layer framework** that describes exactly how data moves from device to device.
**For a hacker, each layer = a different attack surface.** Every protocol, exploit, and defence tool maps to a specific OSI layer.
When you hear 'Layer 7 DDoS attack' or 'Layer 3 routing issue' — that is the OSI model in real professional use.
**Knowing OSI = speaking the language of networking.** Without it you are guessing. With it you know exactly where to attack and defend.

slides.time: 30 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 Playlist 1–3

slides.label: OSI Layers 1–3
slides.heading: Physical · Data Link · **Network**
slides.note: **Layer 2** → target of ARP spoofing (MITM attacks). **Layer 3** → target of IP spoofing and routing attacks.

slides.label: OSI Layers 4–5
slides.heading: Transport & __Session__ Layers

body.text: **Layer 4 — Transport:** TCP vs UDP. Port numbers live here. Nmap port scanning, SYN flood attacks, and TCP handshake exploits all operate at this layer.
**TCP** — reliable, ordered, connection-based. Used by HTTP, SSH, FTP. Vulnerable to SYN flood DoS attacks.
**UDP** — fast, connectionless. Used by DNS, VoIP. Target of UDP amplification DDoS attacks.
**Layer 5 — Session:** Manages connection states. Session hijacking attacks target this layer.

slides.time: 20 min · TryHackMe 'Intro to Networking'

slides.label: OSI Layers 6–7
slides.heading: Presentation & __Application__

body.text: **Layer 6 — Presentation:** Encryption (SSL/TLS), data encoding, and format conversion. HTTPS protects your data here.
**Layer 7 — Application:** HTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP — everything you touch daily. This is where **most attacks happen:** SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, and phishing all live here.
Web application hacking (later in this series) operates almost entirely at **Layer 7** — the richest attack surface in cybersecurity.

slides.time: 20 min · Cloudflare OSI Learning Center

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 1 Checklist
steps: Name all **7 OSI layers** in order — Physical to Application
Know which protocols belong to each layer — HTTP=L7, TCP=L4, IP=L3
Understand **TCP vs UDP** and why it matters for hacking
Memorise the mnemonic: **Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away** (layers 1→7)
Map **one attack type** to each layer — this is how pros think
slides.time: Total Day 1 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes

slides.label: Where to Learn
slides.method: WHERE — Best Free Resources for Today
slides.heading: Learn From The Best — All __Free__

resources.label: 📖 Deep Dive
resources.name: Cloudflare Learning Center — OSI Model Guide

slides.label: Action Plan
slides.heading: Your Hands-On __Task__ for Day 1
steps: **Watch** Prof. Messer N10-009 Playlist 1–3 (60 min) — take notes as you go
**Draw** the OSI model from memory — 7 layers, without looking
**Label** each layer with a real protocol: HTTP (L7), TCP (L4), IP (L3), Ethernet (L2)
**Complete** TryHackMe 'What is Networking?' room — apply everything you just learned
slides.time: Hands-On Task: 30 – 45 minutes

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: Master OSI first — every tool, attack, and protocol you will learn for the next 89 days maps directly back to these 7 layers.
slides.sub: The OSI model is not just theory — it is the mental map professionals use to diagnose problems in seconds. Network+ and OSCP examiners expect you to think in layers.
slides.mistake: Jumping straight to TCP/IP without mastering OSI. TCP/IP is a simplified 4-layer version — skip OSI and you will hit a wall when debugging complex attacks.

cover.title: TCP/IP Model & __IP Addressing__
cover.hook: What's your IP address — and why should every hacker know this?

slides.label: TCP/IP vs OSI
slides.heading: TCP/IP: The Internet's __Real Blueprint__

body.text: While OSI has 7 layers, the real internet runs on **TCP/IP — a practical 4-layer model** used by every device on earth.
**OSI is conceptual. TCP/IP is operational.** It combines OSI's top 3 into Application, keeps Transport, merges Network+Internet, and collapses Physical+Data Link into Network Access.
**For hacking:** Every exploit, payload, and reverse shell travels through TCP/IP. Understanding how packets move is non-negotiable for any penetration tester.

slides.time: 20 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 Playlist

slides.label: IPv4 Basics
slides.heading: IPv4: The __Address System__ of the Internet

body.text: **IPv4** uses 32-bit addresses written in dotted decimal notation — for example: 192.168.1.1. Each of the 4 octets ranges from 0 to 255.
**Public vs Private IPs:** Public IPs are routable on the internet. Private IPs are used inside local networks and never appear on the public internet.
**Why IPv4 is running out:** Only ~4.3 billion addresses exist. With billions of devices online, address exhaustion is a real problem — solved by NAT and IPv6.
**For pentesters:** Knowing public vs private ranges is essential — private IPs reveal internal network structure during assessments.

slides.time: 25 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 IP Addressing Section

slides.label: IPv6 Basics
slides.heading: IPv6: The __Future__ of Addressing

body.text: **IPv6** uses 128-bit hex addresses — written as 2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334. Colons separate 8 groups of 4 hex digits.
**Why it matters:** IPv6 provides **340 undecillion unique addresses** — solving IPv4 exhaustion permanently. Most modern networks run dual-stack (both IPv4 and IPv6).
**Security implication:** Many firewalls and IDS rules are written for IPv4 only. IPv6 traffic on a network is sometimes **unfiltered** — an attack surface pentesters exploit.

slides.time: 20 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 IPv6 Section

slides.label: Private IP Ranges
slides.method: WHAT — Private IP Ranges to Memorise
slides.heading: Private IP __Ranges__ — Memorise These

body.text: 127.0.0.1   = Loopback (localhost)
169.254.x.x = APIPA (no DHCP assigned)

slides.note: These never appear on the public internet. Seeing them in a scan = internal network confirmed.
slides.time: 15 min · Flashcard drill — memorise all 3 ranges

slides.label: Subnetting Intro
slides.heading: Day 2 Checklist
steps: Understand the **4-layer TCP/IP model** and how it maps to OSI
Memorise **IPv4 dotted decimal notation** and the 3 private ranges
Understand **CIDR notation** — /24 = 256 addresses, /16 = 65,536 addresses
Run `ipconfig` (Windows) or `ip a` (Linux) — identify your IP, subnet, gateway
Understand **why subnetting matters** for network segmentation and pentest scoping
slides.time: Total Day 2 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes

resources.label: 📺 Video
resources.name: Prof. Messer N10-009 — IP Addressing Section

slides.label: Action Plan
slides.heading: Your Hands-On __Task__ for Day 2
steps: **Open** terminal — run `ipconfig` (Windows) or `ip a` (Linux) — document your IP and subnet
**Identify** whether each IP is public or private using the 3 private ranges
**Practice** 10 subnetting questions at subnettingpractice.com — use binary, not shortcuts
**Write** a cheat sheet: 5 private ranges, loopback, APIPA — with descriptions
slides.time: Hands-On Task: 45 – 60 minutes

stats.label: Private IP ranges every hacker must know

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: Learn subnetting with binary — not just formulas. When you understand the binary math, subnetting becomes instant and permanent.
slides.sub: Every Network+ exam has subnetting questions. Every pentest scope document uses CIDR notation. Every internal network runs on private ranges. This is foundational knowledge with zero expiry.
slides.mistake: Memorising IP ranges without understanding binary math behind them. Formulas break under pressure. Binary understanding never does — and it transfers directly to real-world network analysis.

cover.title: DNS, DHCP & __Core Protocols__
cover.hook: DNS is the phonebook of the internet — and hackers exploit it daily.

slides.label: DNS Explained
slides.heading: DNS: The Internet's __Phonebook__

body.text: **DNS (Domain Name System)** translates human-readable names like google.com into IP addresses machines understand — for example: 142.250.80.46.
**4 types of DNS servers:** Recursive Resolver (your ISP) → Root Server → TLD Server (.com/.org) → Authoritative Server (the actual answer).
**Without DNS, the internet breaks.** Every website visit, every email sent, every API call starts with a DNS lookup. Compromising DNS = redirecting all traffic.

slides.time: 20 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 DNS Section

slides.label: DNS Attack Types
slides.method: WHY — DNS is a Hacker's Target
slides.heading: DNS Attacks: __3 You Must Know__

body.text: **DNS Spoofing:** Attacker injects fake DNS records — your query for bank.com returns the attacker's IP. You visit a fake site without knowing.
**DNS Cache Poisoning:** Corrupt the resolver's cache so ALL users on that server get redirected — not just you. Affects thousands simultaneously.
**DNS Hijacking:** Attacker changes your router's DNS server setting — all your DNS queries now go through the attacker's controlled server.
**Defence:** DNSSEC digitally signs DNS records. HTTPS ensures you're on the real site even if DNS is poisoned.

slides.time: 25 min · TryHackMe 'DNS in Detail' Room

slides.label: DHCP Explained
slides.heading: DHCP: Automatic __IP Assignment__

body.text: **DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol)** automatically assigns IP address, subnet mask, default gateway, and DNS server to every device that connects to a network.
**DORA Process:** Discover → Offer → Request → Acknowledge. Your device broadcasts, the server responds, you request it, server confirms.
**Attack angle — Rogue DHCP:** An attacker sets up a fake DHCP server on the network. All new devices get assigned the attacker's machine as their **default gateway** — full traffic interception.

slides.time: 20 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 DHCP Section

slides.label: Critical Ports Reference
slides.heading: Critical __Ports__ Every Hacker Knows

body.text: 21  — FTP   (File Transfer — plaintext, insecure)
22  — SSH   (Secure Shell — encrypted remote access)
25  — SMTP  (Email sending — often abused for spam)
53  — DNS   (Domain resolution — UDP + TCP)
80  — HTTP  (Web — plaintext, interceptable)
443 — HTTPS (Web — TLS encrypted)
3389— RDP   (Remote Desktop — common attack target)

slides.time: 30 min · Flashcard drill — all 20 top ports

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 3 Checklist
steps: Explain **how DNS works** end-to-end — the full 4-server lookup chain
Understand **DNS spoofing vs cache poisoning** — the difference and impact
Know the **DORA process** for DHCP — and how a rogue DHCP attack works
Memorise **top 20 protocol ports** — they appear in every CTF and pentest
Run `nslookup google.com` and `nslookup google.com 8.8.8.8` — compare results
slides.time: Total Day 3 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes

resources.label: 📺 Video
resources.name: Prof. Messer N10-009 — DNS & DHCP Section

resources.label: 🛠️ Tool
resources.name: Cisco Packet Tracer — Free Network Simulator

slides.label: Action Plan
slides.heading: Your Hands-On __Task__ for Day 3
steps: **Run** `nslookup google.com` — see which DNS server resolved the query
**Try** `nslookup facebook.com 8.8.8.8` — use Google's public DNS instead
**Run** `ipconfig /all` (Windows) or `cat /etc/resolv.conf` (Linux) — find your DHCP server
**Complete** TryHackMe 'DNS in Detail' room — apply everything learned today
slides.time: Hands-On Task: 45 – 60 minutes

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: Memorise the top 20 port numbers cold. They appear in 80% of exam questions and every single professional port scan output you will ever read.
slides.sub: Build a physical cheat sheet — write each port and protocol by hand, not just typed. Handwriting encodes memory differently. Review it every morning for one week. You will never forget them.
slides.mistake: Confusing IMAP (143) with POP3 (110) — both receive email but differently. IMAP keeps email on server and syncs. POP3 downloads and deletes. This distinction appears in both exams and phishing attack analysis.

cover.title: Network Topologies & __Hardware__
cover.hook: The shape of your network determines how easy it is to hack.

slides.label: Network Topologies
slides.heading: Network __Topology__: Shape = Attack Surface

body.text: A **network topology** defines how devices are physically and logically connected. Different shapes create different security vulnerabilities and failure points.
**Star** — all devices connect to a central switch. Most common. Single point of failure = the switch.
**Bus** — all devices on one cable. Legacy. One break = entire network down.
**Ring** — data travels in a loop. Token Ring (legacy). Predictable but fragile.
**Mesh** — every device connects to every other. Redundant, resilient. Used in critical infrastructure.
**Hybrid** — combination of topologies. Used in all real enterprise environments.

slides.time: 20 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 Topology Section

slides.label: Routers vs Switches
slides.heading: Routers vs Switches: __Critical Difference__

body.text: **Switches (Layer 2):** Connect devices within the same network. Forward frames based on MAC addresses. A compromised switch enables ARP spoofing and VLAN hopping attacks.
**Routers (Layer 3):** Connect different networks together. Forward packets based on IP addresses. A misconfigured router leaks routing tables and enables man-in-the-middle attacks.
**Hubs (Legacy — Layer 1):** Broadcast every packet to every port. No intelligence. Any device connected can capture all traffic with Wireshark — attackers love finding them.

slides.time: 25 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 Hardware Section

slides.label: Firewalls & VLANs
slides.heading: Firewalls & __VLANs__: Defence in Depth

body.text: **Firewall Types:** Packet-filtering (Layer 3/4 rules only), Stateful Inspection (tracks connection state), NGFW/Next-Gen (deep packet inspection, application-aware).
**VLANs (Virtual LANs):** Logically segment a network without physical separation. Finance, HR, and IT can share the same switches but never see each other's traffic.
**VLAN Hopping Attack:** If a switch port is misconfigured as a trunk, an attacker can tag frames to jump between VLANs — bypassing all logical segmentation.

slides.time: 20 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 Firewall Section

slides.label: Wireless Basics
slides.heading: Wireless __Standards__ and Attacks

body.text: WEP  — Broken. Never use. Cracked in minutes with aircrack-ng.
WPA  — Weak. TKIP encryption. Vulnerable to dictionary attacks.
WPA2 — Standard. AES encryption. Vulnerable to PMKID/handshake capture.
WPA3 — Current best. SAE (dragonfly handshake). Resists offline cracking.
802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6) — Fastest current standard. WPA3 mandatory.

slides.note: **Evil Twin Attack:** Create a fake AP with the same SSID. Victims connect automatically. Full traffic interception — no cracking needed.
slides.time: 20 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 Wireless Section

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 4 Checklist
steps: Draw and label **all 5 network topologies** — identify failure points of each
Explain the difference between **router, switch, and hub** at OSI layer level
Understand **VLAN segmentation** and how VLAN hopping attacks bypass it
Know **WEP, WPA, WPA2, WPA3** — why WEP is broken and WPA3 is secure
Build a simple **star topology** in Cisco Packet Tracer — assign IPs, ping between PCs
slides.time: Total Day 4 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes

resources.label: 🛠️ Lab
resources.name: Cisco Packet Tracer — Free Network Simulation

slides.label: Action Plan
slides.heading: Your Hands-On __Task__ for Day 4
steps: **Download** Cisco Packet Tracer (free at netacad.com) — install it today
**Build** a star topology: 1 router, 1 switch, 3 PCs — assign IPs and ping between all PCs
**Draw** each topology type from memory — label failure points in red
**Research** your home router — is it also a switch? What SSID security does it use?
slides.time: Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: Real enterprise networks are always hybrid topologies. Learn each type individually — only then can you understand and attack a combined real-world network.
slides.sub: In a pentest, topology knowledge directly impacts your lateral movement strategy. Star networks have one critical choke point — the core switch. Compromise it and you own the segment. Know the shape, own the network.
slides.mistake: Confusing router and switch roles. Routers separate networks. Switches connect devices within a network. Getting this wrong in an exam or client report is an immediate credibility killer.

cover.title: Ports, Protocols & __Firewall Rules__
cover.hook: 65,535 ports. Hackers know them all. Do you?

slides.label: What Are Ports?
slides.heading: Ports: Virtual __Doors__ Into Every System

body.text: **Ports** are virtual endpoints that allow a single IP address to run multiple services simultaneously. Your computer can serve web pages on port 80, SSH on port 22, and email on port 25 — all at once.
**Three port ranges:** 0–1023 (Well-known, reserved for standard services) · 1024–49151 (Registered, assigned to applications) · 49152–65535 (Dynamic/ephemeral, temporary connections).
**For hackers:** Open ports = attack surface. Every open port is a potential entry point. Nmap reveals them all in seconds.

slides.time: 20 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 Ports Section

slides.label: Top Ports to Memorise
slides.heading: Ports Every __Hacker__ Memorises

body.text: 21   — FTP      · 22  — SSH      · 23  — Telnet
25   — SMTP     · 53  — DNS      · 80  — HTTP
110  — POP3     · 143 — IMAP     · 443 — HTTPS
445  — SMB      · 3306— MySQL    · 3389— RDP
8080 — Alt HTTP · 8443— Alt HTTPS· 27017— MongoDB

slides.note: **Telnet (23) is never acceptable** — transmits credentials in plaintext. Always replaced by SSH. Finding Telnet open = instant critical finding in a pentest report.
slides.time: 30 min · Flashcard drill — all ports + protocols

slides.label: TCP vs UDP
slides.method: WHAT — TCP vs UDP Deep Dive
slides.heading: TCP vs UDP: __Know the Difference__

body.text: **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol):** Connection-based. Uses 3-way handshake (SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK). Guaranteed delivery. Used by: HTTP, HTTPS, SSH, FTP, SMTP.
**UDP (User Datagram Protocol):** Connectionless. Fire-and-forget. No delivery confirmation. Used by: DNS, VoIP, DHCP, TFTP, video streaming.
**Attack implications:** TCP's handshake enables SYN flood DoS. UDP's lack of authentication enables amplification DDoS attacks using DNS or NTP servers as reflectors.

slides.time: 20 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 Transport Layer

slides.label: Firewall ACLs
slides.method: WHAT — How Firewalls Use Port Rules
slides.heading: Firewall __ACLs__ and Port Filtering

body.text: **ACLs (Access Control Lists)** define which traffic is allowed or denied based on source IP, destination IP, port, and protocol.
ALLOW  TCP  any → 10.0.0.1:443   (HTTPS in)
ALLOW  TCP  any → 10.0.0.1:80    (HTTP in)
DENY   TCP  any → 10.0.0.1:3389  (Block RDP)
DENY   ALL  any → any             (Implicit deny)
**Implicit deny at the end** is critical — anything not explicitly allowed is blocked. A missing rule is NOT a permission grant.

slides.time: 20 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 Firewall Section

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 5 Checklist
steps: Memorise **top 15 port numbers** cold — without referencing a cheat sheet
Explain **TCP 3-way handshake** and exactly why SYN floods exploit it
Understand **firewall ACL implicit deny** — what happens to unlisted traffic
Run `nmap -sV localhost` — identify every open port on your own machine
Build a **port number cheat sheet** — review it daily for one week
slides.time: Total Day 5 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes

resources.label: 📖 Quiz
resources.name: ExamCompass — Free Port Number Practice Quiz

slides.label: Action Plan
slides.heading: Your Hands-On __Task__ for Day 5
steps: **Install** Nmap (nmap.org) — run `nmap -sV localhost` and document every open port
**Identify** what service is running on each open port — is any unexpected port open?
**Take** ExamCompass port quiz — score yourself and note every wrong answer
**Write** a firewall ACL from scratch — allow HTTPS, block Telnet, implicit deny all
slides.time: Hands-On Task: 45 – 60 minutes

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: Build a cheat sheet of the top 30 ports with protocol and service name. Review it every morning for one week. After that you will never need to look them up again.
slides.sub: In a real pentest, the first Nmap scan result you read will have 20+ open ports. You must recognise each one instantly — what service it is, what version matters, and what attacks target it. Speed of recognition is the skill.
slides.mistake: Scanning any network you do not own or have written permission to scan. Practice only on TryHackMe, HackTheBox, your own VM lab, or localhost. Unauthorised scanning is illegal in most countries — even from your own home.

cover.title: VPNs, NAT & __Network Security__
cover.hook: VPNs hide your traffic — but do you actually know HOW?

slides.label: VPN Types
slides.heading: VPN: __Encrypted Tunnels__ Explained

body.text: A **VPN (Virtual Private Network)** creates an encrypted tunnel between your device and a remote server — making your traffic appear to originate from the VPN server, not your real IP.
**Remote Access VPN:** Individual user → corporate network. Work from home securely.
**Site-to-Site VPN:** Office A ↔ Office B. Both LANs appear as one seamless network.
**SSL VPN:** Browser-based. No client software needed. Common for contractor access.
**VPN Protocols:** IPSec (fastest, most secure), OpenVPN (open source, reliable), WireGuard (modern, ultra-fast), L2TP/IPSec (legacy, widely supported).

slides.time: 20 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 VPN Section

slides.label: NAT Explained
slides.heading: NAT: How Your Router __Shares One IP__

body.text: **NAT (Network Address Translation)** maps your private IP addresses to a single public IP address. Your router does this for every device on your home network.
**Static NAT:** One private IP → one public IP. 1:1 mapping. Used for servers needing a fixed public address.
**Dynamic NAT:** Pool of public IPs shared across private addresses. Assigned on demand.
**PAT (Port Address Translation):** Many private IPs → one public IP using unique port numbers. What your home router actually does.
**Security benefit of NAT:** Internal hosts are not directly reachable from the internet — providing implicit protection (though not a replacement for a firewall).

slides.time: 25 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 NAT Section

slides.label: IDS vs IPS
slides.heading: IDS vs IPS: __Detect vs Prevent__

body.text: **IDS (Intrusion Detection System):** Monitors network traffic, detects suspicious patterns, and **alerts** security teams. It does NOT block traffic. Think of it as a burglar alarm.
**IPS (Intrusion Prevention System):** Everything an IDS does, plus it actively **blocks and drops** malicious traffic in real-time. Think of it as a security guard with a lock.
**Placement matters:** IDS is typically placed after the firewall (out-of-band monitoring). IPS must be inline — between the internet and your network — to block traffic before it arrives.

slides.time: 20 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 IDS/IPS Section

slides.label: DMZ & Zero Trust
slides.heading: DMZ & __Zero Trust__ Architecture

body.text: **DMZ (Demilitarized Zone):** A network segment that sits between the public internet and the internal LAN. Web servers, email servers, and DNS servers live here — publicly accessible but isolated from internal systems.
**Architecture:** Internet → Firewall → DMZ (Web/Email/DNS Server) → Internal Firewall → Internal LAN. Two firewalls = two barriers an attacker must breach.
**Zero Trust Model:** 'Never trust, always verify.' Every user, every device, every request must authenticate — even inside the network. No implicit trust based on network location.

slides.time: 20 min · NIST Zero Trust Architecture Guide

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 6 Checklist
steps: Explain the difference between **Remote Access and Site-to-Site VPN**
Understand **PAT vs Static NAT** — how your home router uses PAT daily
Know the difference between **IDS and IPS** — placement and function
Draw a **DMZ network diagram** — Internet → Firewall → DMZ → Internal Firewall → LAN
Explain **Zero Trust** in 2 sentences — this appears in every senior security interview
slides.time: Total Day 6 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes

resources.label: 📺 Video
resources.name: Prof. Messer N10-009 — VPN, NAT & Security

slides.label: Action Plan
slides.heading: Your Hands-On __Task__ for Day 6
steps: **Draw** a full network diagram: Internet → Firewall → DMZ (Web Server) → Internal Firewall → LAN
**Label** each zone: public, DMZ, internal — and which services belong in each
**Research** your home router's NAT settings — what type of NAT is it using?
**Explain** Zero Trust to someone (or write it down) in your own words — no notes
slides.time: Hands-On Task: 45 – 60 minutes

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: In every job interview for any security role, expect to explain DMZ and IDS vs IPS. These two concepts are asked constantly — get them cold.
slides.sub: Zero Trust is not just a buzzword — it is the current direction of enterprise security architecture. Every major cloud provider (AWS, Azure, GCP) builds Zero Trust products. Understanding the model now puts you ahead of 90% of candidates.
slides.mistake: Thinking VPN = full anonymity. A VPN hides your traffic from your ISP and shifts trust to the VPN provider. It does NOT make you anonymous, does not protect against malware, and does not hide you from determined nation-state adversaries.

cover.title: Network+ Week __Recap__ & Exam Tips
cover.hook: 7 Days of Networking done. Here's everything you need to PASS Network+.

slides.label: Week 1 Recap
slides.heading: Week 1: __Everything You Learned__

body.text: **Day 1:** OSI 7-Layer Model — physical to application, attack surface per layer
**Day 2:** TCP/IP 4-Layer Model — IPv4/IPv6, subnetting, CIDR notation
**Day 3:** DNS, DHCP, Core Protocols — top 20 ports, DORA process, DNS attacks
**Day 4:** Topologies & Hardware — routers, switches, firewalls, VLANs, wireless security
**Day 5:** Ports, Protocols, Firewall ACLs — TCP vs UDP, implicit deny, Nmap scanning
**Day 6:** VPN, NAT, IDS/IPS, DMZ, Zero Trust — modern security architecture

slides.time: 30 min · Review all 6 days of notes

slides.label: N10-009 Exam Format
slides.heading: CompTIA Network+ __N10-009__ Exam

body.text: **Questions:** Up to 90 questions — multiple choice + performance-based simulations
**Time limit:** 90 minutes — approximately 1 minute per question
**Passing score:** 720 out of 900 — approximately 80%
**Key domains:** Networking Concepts (23%) · Network Implementation (20%) · Network Operations (17%) · Network Security (20%) · Network Troubleshooting (20%)
**Performance-based questions (PBQs)** simulate real-world tasks — configuring firewalls, subnetting, troubleshooting connectivity. Do NOT skip them — they are worth more points.

slides.time: 15 min · Read CompTIA official exam objectives

slides.label: Top Exam Topics
slides.heading: Top __Exam Topics__ You Must Master

body.text: **Subnetting calculations** — CIDR notation, usable hosts, network/broadcast addresses. Always on the exam.
**Port numbers** — top 20 ports cold. TCP vs UDP per protocol. Appears in 3–5 questions minimum.
**Cable types** — Cat5e vs Cat6 vs Cat6a. Max length: 100m (Ethernet). Fiber types: single-mode vs multi-mode.
**Wireless standards** — 802.11a/b/g/n/ac/ax speeds, frequencies, WPA2 vs WPA3.
**Troubleshooting methodology** — Top-down (L7→L1) vs Bottom-up (L1→L7). Know both approaches.

slides.time: 45 min · Review CompTIA N10-009 Exam Objectives PDF

slides.label: Study Resources
slides.method: WHERE — Best Free Exam Prep Resources
slides.heading: Best __Free__ Network+ Prep Resources
steps: **Professor Messer** — Free N10-009 video course + study guide PDF at professormesser.com
**ExamCompass** — Free practice quizzes by domain topic at examcompass.com
**Jason Dion** — Udemy course (frequently on sale for $15) — best paid option
**Mike Meyers** — All-in-One Network+ textbook — comprehensive physical reference
**TryHackMe Pre-Security Path** — free hands-on lab practice for networking fundamentals
slides.time: Take a full practice exam today — 90 questions, timed

slides.label: Exam Study Strategy
slides.method: HOW — How to Pass Network+ First Try
slides.heading: Day 7 Checklist
steps: Take a **full 90-question practice test** at ExamCompass — timed, no cheating
Write down **every wrong answer topic** — that becomes your targeted study list
**Review your notes** from Days 1–6 — fill any gaps identified by the practice test
Understand the **troubleshooting methodology** — top-down vs bottom-up OSI approach
**Schedule your exam** if you feel ready — commit to a date, it drives urgency
slides.time: Total Day 7 Study Time: 120 – 150 minutes

resources.label: 📺 Full Course

slides.label: Action Plan
slides.heading: Your Hands-On __Task__ for Day 7
steps: **Take** a full ExamCompass N10-009 practice test — 90 questions, 90 minutes, no notes
**Score** yourself honestly — anything below 75% means a topic needs more attention
**Review** all 6 days of notes — create a single one-page master reference sheet
**Post** your progress online with #Day7of90 — accountability accelerates learning
slides.time: Hands-On Task: 90 – 120 minutes

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: Network+ is the foundation for EVERYTHING in cybersecurity. Do not rush it. Every advanced topic — pentesting, cloud security, threat hunting — builds on what you learned this week.
slides.sub: The study method that works: Watch video → Take notes by hand → Practice questions → Review wrong answers → Repeat. Do NOT just watch. Active recall through practice questions is what makes knowledge stick permanently.
slides.mistake: Skipping subnetting practice because it seems hard. Subnetting questions appear on every Network+ exam. Students who skip it fail — without exception. Do 20 subnetting problems a day until it feels automatic.
